text.title: ruby-rails-patterns Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for ruby-rails-patterns as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Service has multiple public methods (call, create, update) or no call method at all.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Breaks single-responsibility; unclear which method is the entry point; harder to compose with organizers. A service is a command object with exactly one entry point.

failure-mode.description: rescue StandardError inside the service, swallowing all exceptions into ServiceResult.failure.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Hides real bugs (NoMethodError, TypeError) as domain failures. Only catch documented domain exceptions like ActiveRecord::RecordInvalid or custom exceptions.

failure-mode.description: Service returns raw ActiveRecord object on success, exception on failure — two divergent paths.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Controllers must handle both paths differently; ServiceResult enforces a uniform interface.

failure-mode.description: Services reference params.require(...) or params.permit(...) from the controller. The service couples to Rails ActionController::Parameters.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Couples the service to Rails and prevents unit testing without mocking params. Services accept already-permitted plain hashes from the controller, not ActionController::Parameters.

failure-mode.description: Services call deliver_now or perform_now for mailer/job dispatch, causing service specs to send real emails or run jobs synchronously.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Synchronous dispatch makes tests slow, side-effect-prone, and brittle. Always use deliver_later / perform_later so the mailer/job is enqueued, not executed immediately.
